title: SEGMENT 3 — James Allison and the discovery of CTLA-4 as a brake

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — James Allison and the discovery of CTLA-4 as a brake
Let me tell you about James Allison.
It is the early 1990s.
Allison is a Texan immunologist, born in Alice, Texas, country-music-loving, harmonica-playing, by all accounts not the typical buttoned-down Nobel laureate type.
He's working on a molecule called CTLA-4, which stands for cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4 — a name that, helpfully, tells you almost nothing useful.
CTLA-4 had been cloned in 1987 by a French group, and it was known to appear on the surface of activated T cells, but its function was murky.
The dominant view at the time was that CTLA-4 was probably an accelerator — a second signal that revved T cells up, similar to a closely related molecule called CD28.
CD28 was already known to be the gas pedal of T-cell activation.
People thought CTLA-4 was a backup gas pedal.
But Allison and a few others — notably Jeffrey Bluestone and Arlene Sharpe, who deserve more credit than the popular story usually gives them — started to suspect something stranger.
Maybe CTLA-4 wasn't an accelerator at all.
Maybe it was a brake.
Here's the logic.
T cells, when they get activated, undergo a kind of two-key activation process.
The first key is recognition of the antigen — the T cell receptor binds an MHC-peptide complex on an antigen-presenting cell.
The second key is a co-stimulatory signal — CD28 on the T cell engages a molecule called B7 on the antigen-presenting cell.
Both signals together, the T cell goes from zero to sixty and starts dividing, producing cytokines, mounting an attack.
One signal alone, the T cell does nothing — or worse, becomes tolerant, lazy, anergic.
Now, CTLA-4 appears on the T cell's surface only after the T cell has already been activated.
It comes up late.
And it binds the same B7 molecule that CD28 binds — but with much higher affinity.
Much, much higher.
So as the T cell ramps up its response, CTLA-4 starts appearing and starts outcompeting CD28 for the B7 ligand.
Whatever B7 is doing through CD28 to keep the T cell revved up, CTLA-4 starts stealing the substrate.
Allison's group did the experiment that mattered.
They took mice with tumors.
They made an antibody against CTLA-4 — an antibody that physically blocked CTLA-4 from binding B7.
They injected it.
And the tumors regressed.
Let me say that again.
They injected an antibody that blocked a single T-cell surface molecule, and the mice's immune systems destroyed their own tumors.
This was a 1996 paper in the journal Science.
It was, at the time, almost unbelievable.
The dogma had been that the immune system was, basically, irrelevant to cancer.
You couldn't shrink tumors by tickling the immune system.
People had tried.
It hadn't worked.
And here was a single antibody making colon tumors and fibrosarcomas vanish in mice.
The model Allison and his colleagues built from this was beautiful and clear.
CTLA-4 is a brake.
It is a molecule that emerges on activated T cells specifically to slow them down — to prevent runaway responses that would damage healthy tissue.
In normal life, that brake is critical.
Without it, you get fatal autoimmunity — and indeed, mice in which the CTLA-4 gene is deleted develop massive lymphoproliferative disease and die within weeks.
The brake is essential.
But in the context of cancer, the brake is the enemy.
The T cells are trying to attack a tumor.
The brake is engaging and shutting them down.
Block the brake — temporarily, with an antibody — and the T cells get to keep going.
The brake-cutter, if you will, frees the immune system to do what it was already trying to do.
Allison spent the rest of the 1990s trying to convince a pharmaceutical industry that did not want to listen.
The standard view was still: immune therapy doesn't work, why are you wasting our time.
He shopped his idea around.
Eventually, a small biotech called Medarex — which had a platform for making fully human antibodies — agreed to take a chance.
They developed an anti-human-CTLA-4 antibody, eventually called ipilimumab.
Bristol-Myers Squibb bought Medarex.
They put ipilimumab into trials for advanced metastatic melanoma — at the time, one of the most universally lethal cancers in adult medicine.
Median survival, six to nine months.
No drug had ever shown a survival benefit.
In 2010, the trial results came out.
Patients given ipilimumab lived longer.
Not all of them — only about twenty percent had durable responses — but those who did respond were, in some cases, still alive years later.
In a few cases, decades later.
People with metastatic melanoma simply did not survive ten years.
With ipilimumab, some of them did.
In March 2011, the FDA approved ipilimumab — brand name Yervoy — for advanced melanoma.
It was the first immune checkpoint inhibitor ever approved.
The era began.
We'll come back to Yervoy specifically in Segment 5.
For now, the takeaway is the principle.
Allison's insight: CTLA-4 is a brake on T cells.
Block the brake, and the T cells fight cancer.
That insight, eventually, won him half of the 2018 Nobel Prize.
The other half went to a man who had been working, completely independently, on a second brake, on the other side of the Pacific.
